perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" means copyright, trade secret, patent, and all other rights of a similar nature;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" means copyright, but excludes trade secrets, patents, and all other rights of a similar nature;
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding trade secrets and patents from the definition of 'Intellectual Property Rights,' the contract creates a contradiction. The term Intellectual Property Rights is typically understood to include all of the categories listed. This could lead to disputes over ownership and protection of trade secrets and patented material deposited in escrow.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Act of 1996 (18 U.S. Code § 1839) and Patent Act (35 U.S.C. § 101)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets Act of 1996 protects trade secrets, and the Patent Act (35 U.S.C. § 101) protects inventions and discoveries. By excluding trade secrets and patents from the definition of Intellectual Property Rights, the contract contradicts the legal recognition and protection afforded to these types of intellectual property under United States law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process , machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1930, ch. 312, § 1, 46 Stat. 376 , (3) Aug. 5, 1939, ch. 450, § 1, 53 Stat. 1212 ). The corresponding section of existing statute is split into two sections, section 101 relating to the subject matter for which patents may be obtained, and section 102 defining statutory novelty and stating other conditions for patentability. Section 101 follows the wording of the existing statute as to the subject matter for patents, except that reference to plant patents has been omitted for incorporation in section 301 and the word “art” has been replaced by “ process ”, which is defined in section 100. The word “art” in the corresponding section of the existing statute has a different meaning than the same word as used in other places in the statute; it has been interpreted by the courts as being practically synonymous with process or method. “ Process ” has been used as its meaning is more readily grasped than “art” as interpreted, and the definition in section 100(b) makes it clear that “ process or method” is meant. The remainder of the definition clarifies the status of processes or methods which involve merely the new use of a known process , machine, manufacture, composition of matter, or material; they are processes or methods under the statute and may be patented provided the conditions for patentability are sa
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes trade secrets and patents from the definition of "Intellectual Property Rights," directly contradicting the legal recognition and protection afforded to these types of intellectual property under United States law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 35 U.S. Code § 101, indicates that inventors of new and useful processes, machines, manufactures, or compositions of matter may obtain a patent. The original text included patents within the definition of Intellectual Property Rights, aligning with legal norms. The changed text reverses this, creating a direct conflict with established legal protections for patents.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1.1 the Owner enters into any composition or arrangement with its creditors or (being a company) enters into liquidation whether compulsory or voluntary (other than for the purposes of solvent reconstruction or amalgamation) or has a receiver or administrative receiver appointed over all or any part of its assets or undertaking or a petition is presented for an Administration Order or (being an individual or partnership) becomes bankrupt, or an event occurs within the jurisdiction of the country in which the Owner is situated which has a similar effect to any of the above events in the United Kingdom; or
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1.1 the Owner enters into any composition or arrangement with its creditors or (being a company) enters into liquidation whether compulsory or voluntary (for the purposes of solvent reconstruction or amalgamation) or has a receiver or administrative receiver appointed over all or any part of its assets or undertaking or a petition is presented for an Administration Order or (being an individual or partnership) becomes bankrupt, or an event occurs within the jurisdiction of the country in which the Owner is situated which has a similar effect to any of the above events in the United Kingdom; or
perturbation.explanation: The original text excludes 'solvent reconstruction or amalgamation' from triggering the release of material. By removing 'other than for the purposes of', this means that even if the Owner enters into liquidation for solvent reconstruction or amalgamation, the Licensee is entitled to the Material. However, this contradicts the original intent to exclude such circumstances, which leads to uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United Kingdom Insolvency Act 1986 (Specifically, sections dealing with administration and liquidation)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Insolvency Act 1986 (UK) defines and regulates insolvency procedures, including liquidation. The modification alters the contractual understanding of when liquidation triggers the escrow release, potentially conflicting with how the Act intends to treat solvent restructurings. The original intention was to prevent liquidations for reorganization from triggering the release, and the added text is in contradiction of that intention.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent of the contract by removing the exclusion for 'solvent reconstruction or amalgamation' from triggering the release of material. The original contract specifically stated '(other than for the purposes of solvent reconstruction or amalgamation)' implying that such actions should not trigger the release. By removing 'other than for the purposes of', the modified text now includes these actions as triggers, going against the contract's initial purpose and potentially conflicting with the treatment of solvent restructurings as intended by the Insolvency Act 1986, as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from England and Wales to Delaware introduces potential conflicts. Many provisions are based on English law (e.g. Administration Order, Receiver). Applying Delaware law to concepts defined under English law creates uncertainty and enforceability issues because Delaware Law would not recognize these terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware General Corporation Law governs corporations formed in Delaware. The original contract relies on legal concepts specific to England and Wales. By changing the governing law to Delaware, the contract creates a conflict because Delaware law does not recognize or define terms such as 'Administration Order' and 'Receiver' in the same way, rendering those provisions ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.

Miscellaneous Provisions
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from England and Wales to Delaware introduces a contradiction because the original contract includes terms like 'Administration Order' and 'Receiver' that are specific to English law. Delaware law, as suggested by Snippet 1 which references 'Insolvency; Receivers and Trustees' under Delaware's General Corporation Law, may address receivership but it will not recognize the English terms or have an equivalent framework to the original contract. Therefore, applying Delaware law to these provisions renders them ambiguous and potentially unenforceable, which directly contradicts the purpose and enforceability of those clauses. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions.
